payload.summary: Verified all four amendments against the actual codebase. Three are genuine bugs deserving fixes: fr-9-170e6cf9 (BackendUnavailable classification silently discarded by map_invoke_error catch-all), fr-9-84798097 (blank OPENROUTER_API_KEY accepted and error misclassified as retryable), and fr-9-466cef86 (unnecessary 5s delay on first retry). One (fr-9-2de499b0) is a valid observation about cleanup layering but not a defect given the single-callsite reality.

votes.rationale: Verified that invoke() is the sole caller of spawn_and_wait() (two call sites at lines 1219 and 1259, both within invoke()). Both call sites correctly handle the Timeout failure_class by calling preserve_failed_artifacts() immediately after (lines 1230-1233 and 1270-1276). The cleanup contract — spawn_and_wait() owns process lifecycle, invoke() owns artifact lifecycle — is architecturally sound. The amendment's concern is purely hypothetical: a future caller that forgets to handle Timeout artifacts. That risk doesn't justify restructuring the ownership model. spawn_and_wait() doesn't own PreparedCommand and shouldn't be responsible for artifact preservation. A doc comment on the contract would be a minor nicety, not an amendment-worthy change.

Confirmed: cursor.attempt is initialized to 1 (domain.rs:768), backoff_for_attempt(1) computes 5 * 2^(1-1) = 5 seconds (retry_policy.rs:110-111), and this call at engine.rs:4620 happens before cursor.retry() increments the counter at line 4661. The first retry always incurs a full 5-second wait. For common transient failures like JSON parse errors on partial responses, this is gratuitous latency. Using backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt - 1) would hit the attempt==0 guard (retry_policy.rs:108) returning Duration::ZERO, giving an immediate first retry while preserving the exponential ramp (5s, 10s, 20s, ...) for repeated failures. This is a standard backoff pattern and a clear improvement to p50 retry latency.

Confirmed the bug in service.rs:383-410. map_invoke_error matches InvocationFailed and InvocationTimeout/InvocationCancelled variants explicitly (lines 390-402), but BackendUnavailable falls into the catch-all 'other' arm (line 403) and gets converted to InvocationFailed { failure_class: TransportFailure }. This silently discards the BinaryNotFound classification that openrouter_backend.rs:74-78 and process_backend.rs:540-549 (via resolve_binary) deliberately set. The consequence is concrete: a missing codex binary or missing API key at invoke-time gets classified as a retryable transport failure, burning through every retry attempt before failing — adding minutes of pointless delay to what should be an immediate terminal error. The fix is straightforward: add a BackendUnavailable arm that preserves or maps the failure_class.

Confirmed three distinct issues: (1) process_backend.rs:802 uses std::env::var().map_err() which only catches unset variables — OPENROUTER_API_KEY="" passes the check and sends an empty key, failing later with an unhelpful transport error; (2) the error is classified as TransportFailure (retryable) at line 805, directly contradicting check_availability() at lines 1200-1210 which uses BackendUnavailable{BinaryNotFound} (terminal) with the correct .unwrap_or_default().is_empty() pattern; (3) openrouter_backend.rs:73 uses Ok(value) if !value.trim().is_empty() — the correct approach. The test at process_backend_test.rs:1393-1421 asserts InvocationFailed, encoding the buggy behavior. Fix: mirror check_availability's pattern and return BackendUnavailable{BinaryNotFound}, update the test expectation.
